perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of approximately five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will endeavor not to disseminate the confidential information except to its employees involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use what it considers a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'approximately five (5) years' introduces ambiguity to the duration of confidentiality. Replacing 'will not disseminate' with 'will endeavor not to disseminate' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care)' with 'what it considers a reasonable degree of care' gives RECIPIENT subjective discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, but the modified language makes the standard of care subjective ('what it considers a reasonable degree of care'), which is likely insufficient and therefore contradicts the requirement to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information. While Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' the changed text introduces 'approximately five years' and 'what it considers a reasonable degree of care,' making the duty less strict and more subjective. This could be interpreted as a breach, since the recipient now has more discretion and the obligation to maintain secrecy is weakened, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: Information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO should generally remain the property of HALO, and the written information, and any copies thereof, should be returned to HALO upon written request or disposed of by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'should generally remain' and 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should be returned' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity regarding ownership and return of information. Removing 'at HALO's option' takes away HALO's control over the destruction of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The ambiguous phrasing around returning the information upon request contradicts CUTSA because it doesn't guarantee the information will be protected, or that HALO will be able to obtain it back promptly or control the process of destroying it.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens obligations related to trade secret protection, creating a contradiction. The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a firm requirement for information ownership and return. The changed text uses 'should generally,' introducing ambiguity and discretion. This weakens the requirement to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1 from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the obligation to return information promptly and control its destruction, the changed text increases the risk of a breach, thus contradicting the requirement to maintain secrecy as required to maintain trade secret status under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally governed by the principles of law of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of California. The modified text uses the phrase 'generally governed by the principles of law,' which is vague and could be interpreted to mean that other legal principles could apply, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. The changed text creates ambiguity as to whether California law fully applies. This contradicts the specific intent that California law governs the contract. By specifying it is only generally governed, it opens to interpretations that may not conform with the specific laws of California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text firmly establishes that the agreement adheres to California law. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement is 'generally governed by the principles of law' of California. This weakens the original commitment and allows for interpretations outside California's specific statutory laws, potentially conflicting with them. Snippet 1 from the California Civil Code (CIV 1646) states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed or made, which aligns with the original text's intent. The change undermines this by creating uncertainty whether the contract is entirely governed by California law.

perturbation.original_text: HALO Agrees to transmit to RECIPIENT confidential information for evaluation purposes only.
perturbation.changed_text: HALO intends to transmit to RECIPIENT confidential information related to evaluation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Agrees to transmit' to 'intends to transmit' weakens the commitment, making the transmission of confidential information discretionary rather than obligatory. Removing 'for evaluation purposes only' broadens the scope of potential use of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the explicit intention behind the confidential information being transmitted. By weakening the agreement, it makes it more difficult for HALO to demonstrate that it took reasonable measures to protect its trade secrets, because this clause established the expectation and agreement that HALO will, in fact, transmit confidential information. Similarly, by weakening the provision stating 'evaluation purposes only', this removes an important measure to protect the secret, undermining the CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Agrees to transmit' to 'intends to transmit' weakens the obligation to share confidential information, and removing 'for evaluation purposes only' broadens the scope of potential use of the information, undermining the protection of trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The original text provided a clear obligation and limited purpose, supporting a claim of trade secret protection. The modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially weakening HALO's ability to prove they took reasonable steps to protect their trade secrets under California law.
